content.full_text:
State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
File 
Date 
Jan. 2 8, 19 7 7 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Ken Finney 
Subject: Telephone conversation with David Fitzsimons, Assistant 
City Attorney for the City of Modesto, and Charlene Bomar, 
an attorney in the City Attorney's office. 
On January 27, 1977, I received a call from Assistant City 
Attorney Fitzsimonsrequesting advice with respect to potential 
conflicts of interests on the part of a new planning 
commis-
sioner in the City of Modesto. 
Mr. Fitzsimons related the 
following facts. 
The new planning commissioner is the 
secretary-treasurer of the Carpenters' Union and also the 
secretary-treasurer for the Building Trades Council, which 
is an amalgamation of various construction units in the City 
of Modesto. 
The planning commissioner works three-and-a-half 
days for the Carpenters' Union and one-and-a-half days for 
the Building Trades Council. 
He is paid by the Carpenters' 
Union, but the Building Trades Cound:i: reimburses th(~Carpenters' 
Union for the portion of his salary attributable to the one-
and-a-half days that he devotes to the Building Trades Council. 
In Modesto, approximately 75% of all commercial construction 
is done by unionized labor. 
On the other hand, almost all of 
the residential construction is done by non-union labor. 
Mr. Fitzsimons inquired about what types of conflicts the 
planning commissioner would have when the planning commission 
considered various projects for the City of Modesto, particu-
larly commercial projects that would create work for union 
members. 
I indicated to Mr. Fitzsimons that I felt the 
Carpenters' Union and the Building Trades Council were both 
sources of income to the planning commissioner and that, 
accordingly, potential conflicts did exist with respect to 
both of those entities. 
I also indicated, on the other hand, 
that I did not think the individual members of the Union or 
of the Building Trades Council were sources of income. 
In 
other words, I drew a distinction between the Carpenters' 
Union and the Building Trades Council which I believe 
are 
independent entities and the members of those entities; and 
I concluded that the sources of income to the planning com-
missioner are the Union and the Council, not the members of 
those organizations. 


File 
p.2 
Jan. 28, 1977 
Based on this analysis, I then indicated to Mr. Fitzsimons 
that a conflict would exist if it were reasonably foreseeable 
that any decision of the planning commission would have a 
material financial effect on either of the entities. 
For ex-
ample, Mr. Fitzsimons indicated that certain projects could 
increase the number of union members and hence the amount 
of dues collected by the Carpenters' Union. 
I indicated that 
if the increase in income to 
the Union were material, this 
could create a conflict of interest for the planning commissioner. 
On the other hand, I indicated that if a decision merely created 
more business for union carpenters but did not affect the Car-
penters' Union itself, I did not think there would be a prohibited 
conflict of interest. 
I acknowledged that the planning commis-
sioner obviously would be biased in favor of the Union members 
but I suggested that this would not be a type of bias which is 
prohibited by the Political Reform Act. 
Mr. Fitzsimons then provided me with a series of actual exam-
ples of issues pending before the planning commission. 
I dis-
cussed these examples in general terms but stated that I would 
need more factual information to reach any type of definitive 
conclusion with respect to a conflict of interest. 
I did, 
however, attempt to reiterate what I thought were the criteria 
that would be involved in deciding whether a conflict existed. 
I then encouraged Mr. Fitzsimons to seek a formal opinion and 
to provide us with all the necessary facts so that the staff 
could review the situations about which he was inquiring. 
He 
indicated that he might provide such an opinion request. 
After speaking to Mr. Fitzsimons, I spoke to Ted Prim about 
my conversation. 
Ted indicated that he and Ken Goshorn had 
discussed an analagous situation and had concluded that the 
members of the Union might be sources of income to the planning 
commissioner and that they might disagree with my analysis. 
Of course, if Ted is correct, then a decision which merely 
benefited the members of the Union, even if it did not benefit 
the Union itself, could create a prohibited conflict. 
KF:jo

that would be involved in deciding whether a conflict existed. 
I then encouraged Mr. Fitzsimons to seek a formal opinion and 
to provide us with all the necessary facts so that the staff 
could review the situations about which he was inquiring. 
He 
indicated that he might provide such an opinion request. 
After speaking to Mr. Fitzsimons, I spoke to Ted Prim about 
my conversation. 
Ted indicated that he and Ken Goshorn had 
discussed an analagous situation and had concluded that the 
members of the Union might be sources of income to the planning 
commissioner and that they might disagree with my analysis. 
Of course, if Ted is correct, then a decision which merely

sections.question_synthetic: What conflicts of interest arise when a city planning commissioner is the secretary-treasurer of both the Carpenters' Union and Building Trades Council, which receive material financial benefits from planning commission decisions affecting unionized construction?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Carpenters' Union and Building Trades Council are sources of income and potential conflict sources. A conflict exists if planning decisions could have material financial effects on these entities themselves. However, FPPC staff disagreement exists about whether individual union members are also sources of income that would create conflicts from decisions merely benefiting members without directly benefiting the organizations.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This is a telephone consultation memo, not a formal advice letter. Contains preliminary analysis with internal FPPC disagreement noted. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections present. Contains discussion of staff positions rather than definitive agency conclusion. OCR quality appears good with one apparent OCR error ('Cound:i:' for 'Council').

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What conflicts of interest arise when a city planning commissioner is the secretary-treasurer of both the Carpenters' Union and Building Trades Council, which receive material financial benefits from planning commission decisions affecting unionized construction?

CONCLUSION: with respect to a conflict of interest. 
I did, 
however, attempt to reiterate what I thought were the criteria 
that would be involved in deciding whether a conflict existed. 
I then encouraged Mr. Fitzsimons to seek a formal opinion and 
to provide us with all the necessary facts so that the staff 
could review the situations about which he was inquiring. 
He 
indicated that he might provide such an opinion request. 
After speaking to Mr. Fitzsimons, I spoke to Ted Prim about 
my conversation. 
Ted indicated that he and Ken Goshorn had 
discussed an analagous situation and had concluded that the 
members of the Union might be sources of income to the planning 
commissioner and that they might disagree with my analysis. 
Of course, if Ted is correct, then a decision which merely 
benefited the members of the Union, even if it did not benefit 
the Union itself, could create a prohibited conflict. 
KF:jo

embedding.summary: FPPC staff memo documenting a telephone consultation with Modesto City Attorney regarding potential conflicts of interest for a planning commissioner who is secretary-treasurer of the Carpenters' Union and Building Trades Council. The memo outlines preliminary conflict analysis but notes internal disagreement and recommends the city seek a formal opinion with complete facts.
